author: Dr. Kavita Patel (ED) / Dr. Marcus Hong (Medicine admitting resident) / Dr. Jennifer Beckman (Medicine attending)
content:
ADMISSION HISTORY AND PHYSICAL — JOHNS HOPKINS HOSPITAL
Date/Time: 2/15/2027, 10:30 PM (ED arrival 4:15 PM; admission to medicine accepted 9:45 PM)
Patient: Nguyen, Lan T (MRN: MRN-90215888)
DOB: 9/22/1954 (Age 72)
Admitting team: General Medicine (Dr. Beckman attending, Dr. Hong PGY-3)
Interpreter: Vietnamese language line interpreter used throughout history-taking. In-person Vietnamese interpreter arriving for admission discussion 11 PM.

CHIEF COMPLAINT (per son and family, with Vietnamese interpreter):
'Severe stomach pain and high fever since this morning. She is not herself.'

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Nguyen is a 72-year-old Vietnamese-speaking woman with known cholelithiasis (US-documented 9/2025, surgery deferred), CKD stage 3a, HTN, and HLD, who presents to the ED via car (brought by son Quang and daughter-in-law Linh) with ~16 hours of progressively worsening abdominal pain, fevers, and lethargy.

By patient's account through interpreter (corroborated by son Quang and daughter-in-law Linh, who were with her):
- Approximately 6 AM today, she awoke with severe constant epigastric and right-upper-quadrant abdominal pain, radiating to her right shoulder and back, similar in character to her prior biliary-colic episodes (9/2025 documented; ~3 milder episodes since per family) but MUCH MORE SEVERE and not resolving with rest.
- She had been at a Tết / Lunar New Year family gathering yesterday (2/14) and ate richer foods than usual including pork-belly dishes and coconut-milk-based sweets.
- Within 4 hours of pain onset, she developed nausea and then 3 episodes of non-bilious emesis. Has not been able to keep anything down.
- By noon, family noted she was 'becoming hot' — they did not have a thermometer at home but said she felt feverish and was shivering.
- By 2 PM, family thought she 'looked yellow in the whites of her eyes' (per daughter-in-law).
- By 3:30 PM, son returned from work and found her 'lethargic and confused' on the couch. Family decided to bring her to the ED.
- Denies: hematemesis, melena, BRBPR, current pain anywhere besides abdomen, chest pain, dyspnea, dysuria, hematuria, cough, sore throat, headache, focal weakness, syncope.

PRIOR BILIARY HISTORY:
Ms. Nguyen had a US-documented biliary colic episode 9/30/2025 (Hopkins Bayview UC) with multiple gallstones in a distended GB; no acute cholecystitis at that time. She was referred to General Surgery for elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy (Dr. Markovich, seen 11/14/2025) but elected to defer the surgery, choosing dietary modification instead. She has had at least 1 milder recurrent episode of biliary colic per family in summer 2026 that resolved with rest.

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY (per PCP records, Dr. Phong Le, Charm City Asian Health Center):
- Hyperlipidemia (atorvastatin, LDL at goal)
- Hypertension (lisinopril, currently 5 mg given prior Cr rise)
- Chronic kidney disease, stage 3a (baseline creatinine 1.3, eGFR 47, chronic NSAID-associated likely contributor)
- Cholelithiasis (symptomatic, surgery declined)
- Latent TB infection (positive PPD 14 mm in 2010, positive QuantiFERON; INH treatment declined repeatedly over 16 years; CXR 2010 normal; no symptoms of active TB)
- s/p Right total knee arthroplasty (4/2018, Dr. Reilly, Hopkins Bayview, excellent outcome)
- Left knee osteoarthritis (KL grade 2, conservative)
- Osteopenia (per 2024 DEXA; on calcium + vit D)
- Pre-diabetes (HbA1c 5.8, stable)
- Mild anemia (Hgb chronically 11.5–12.0; CKD-related, low-grade)

PAST SURGICAL HISTORY: Right TKA 4/2018.

MEDICATIONS (per pharmacy reconciliation + son brought bottles):
- Lisinopril 5 mg PO daily
- Atorvastatin 40 mg PO daily
- Acetaminophen 500–1000 mg PO PRN
- Ibuprofen 400–800 mg PO PRN (uses 3–4x/week per PCP note; counseled but continues)
- Calcium 600 mg PO daily + Vitamin D3 1000 IU daily
- Multivitamin daily
- Diclofenac 1% topical gel (left knee, started 10/2026)

ALLERGIES:
- Penicillin (childhood rash, never re-challenged; non-anaphylactic per family report)
- IODINATED CONTRAST — urticarial reaction in 2019 with facial flushing during a CT for knee evaluation; has required pre-medication for subsequent contrast studies (prednisone 50 mg at 13, 7, 1 hr pre + diphenhydramine 50 mg at 1 hr pre). She received emergent oral prednisone in the ED today before CT.

FAMILY HISTORY:
- Father: MI age 68 (1995, Vietnam, untreated HTN)
- Mother: CVA age 81 (2008)
- Older brother: lung cancer (squamous cell) at age 64, died 3/2026 (Vietnam, heavy smoker)
- Younger brother: T2DM, HTN (alive, Vietnam)
- Sister: breast cancer at age 58 (treated, NED, alive)
- Husband Hai: CAD with CABG 2004, died 2018 of HF
- Son Quang (47, here at bedside): hyperlipidemia

SOCIAL HISTORY (per family and patient via interpreter):
- FORMER SMOKER: ~10 cigarettes/d from age 22 (1976) to age 50 (2004); quit 2004 after husband's MI scare. Lifetime ~14 pack-years.
- Alcohol: 1–2 drinks/d (rice wine at dinner, occasional beer). Last drink 2/14/2027 (yesterday, at Tết).
- No illicit drugs.
- Retired since 2021 from Baltimore Textile Co. (Highlandtown garment factory; was floor manager 1992–2021, prior sewing-machine operator 1990–1992; decades of indoor cotton-dust exposure).
- Lives with son Quang, daughter-in-law Linh, two grandchildren (16, 12) in a Charles Village 2nd-floor walk-up apartment. Family is closely involved and at bedside.
- Naturalized US citizen 1998. Immigrated from rural Hà Nam Province, Vietnam, 1990 at age 36.
- Vietnamese language for all healthcare conversations.
- Independent ADLs at baseline. Walks ~1 mile per day. Tai chi 2x/week.
- Insurance: Medicare A+B + Maryland Medicaid (dual-eligible).

ADVANCE DIRECTIVES: No formal advance directive on file. Son Quang has been the verbal medical decision-maker per family discussions; not yet documented. Will revisit.

ED COURSE (per Dr. Patel, ED note):
Arrival 4:15 PM via family. Initial vitals: T 101.3 F (38.5 C), HR 112, BP 98/52, RR 24, SpO2 93% RA. Pain 9/10. Notably tachypneic and somewhat lethargic; oriented to person + family only.
- 4:30 PM: IV access x 2, 1L NS bolus over 30 min. Labs sent. Blood cultures x 2 drawn.
- 4:45 PM: Vitals improved to BP 108/60, HR 102 after fluids.
- 5:15 PM: Labs returned — see below. Lipase 603 (markedly elevated). LFTs with elevated ALP 102 and T-bili 3.2.
- 5:30 PM: CT A/P with contrast ordered (with pre-medication completed in the ED — oral prednisone 50 mg at 5:30 PM was a single dose given allergy history; family expedited consent).
- 6:30 PM: CT A/P with contrast performed (no acute reaction to contrast with pre-medication).
- 7:15 PM: Radiology preliminary read — peripancreatic stranding consistent with acute pancreatitis; cholelithiasis (multiple stones); intrahepatic biliary ductal dilatation. No obvious common bile duct stone visualized but limited evaluation; recommend MRCP if persistent obstruction.
- 7:30 PM: GI consult requested (severe pancreatitis + biliary obstruction) — Dr. Yamamoto (GI fellow) reviewed, recommended ERCP within 24 hours if persistent biliary obstruction; supportive care meanwhile.
- 8:00 PM: Broad-spectrum antibiotics initiated — piperacillin/tazobactam (NB: penicillin allergy mild childhood rash, non-anaphylactic; risk/benefit favored proceeding with pip/tazo given severe sepsis with biliary source — discussed with ID; if Penicillin allergy concern, alternative is meropenem). Patient developed no reaction.
- 9:00 PM: Admission accepted to Medicine. Anticipating ICU upgrade if respiratory or hemodynamic deterioration.

VITALS ON ADMISSION TO MEDICINE FLOOR (10:30 PM):
BP 102/58 (post-fluids), HR 108, RR 22, T 100.6 F, SpO2 94% on 2L NC, weight per family scale ~62 kg. Pain 7/10 on IV morphine 4 mg given in the ED.

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
General: 72-year-old Vietnamese-speaking woman, alert but appears very fatigued and uncomfortable. Mild distress with abdominal pain.
HEENT: Mild scleral icterus bilaterally. Conjunctivae pink. Oropharynx clear; mucous membranes mildly dry. No cervical lymphadenopathy.
CV: Tachycardic 105–110, regular rhythm. No murmur, no rub, no gallop. Pulses 2+ throughout. No peripheral edema.
Lungs: Crackles in both bases. No wheezing. Mild work of breathing. RR 22. SpO2 94% on 2L NC.
Abdomen: Soft. Mildly distended. Tender to palpation in the RUQ and peri-umbilical areas; voluntary guarding without rebound. Murphy sign equivocal. Normal-active bowel sounds. No palpable mass. No flank tenderness.
Extremities: No LE edema. No calf tenderness, no Homan sign.
MSK: Right knee well-healed surgical scar; no acute issues. No joint swelling.
Skin: BCG scar left deltoid. Mild jaundice. No rash, no petechiae.
Neuro: Alert, oriented to person and family but somewhat disoriented to date/place — improving with rehydration. Cranial nerves intact. Strength grossly normal in upper extremities. Sensation intact.

ADMISSION LABS (drawn 4:30 PM):
- See LAB001 panel for full results.
- Highlights: WBC 17.3 (leukocytosis), Hgb 10.2 (down from baseline 11.6), Plt 345. Na 138, K 3.8, Cl 102, CO2 22, BUN 45 (elevated), Cr 1.8 (elevated from baseline 1.3 — AKI on CKD), Glu 132. Total bili 3.2 (elevated), ALP 102 (elevated), AST 78, ALT 92. Lipase 603 (markedly elevated). Lactate 3.2. Albumin 3.4.

IMAGING:
- ABD/PELVIS CT with IV contrast (today, 6:30 PM): see IMG001. Peripancreatic stranding consistent with acute pancreatitis. Cholelithiasis. Intrahepatic biliary ductal dilatation. Spleen normal. Kidneys without hydronephrosis. No abdominal abscess.

IMPRESSION / PLAN:
Ms. Nguyen is a 72-year-old Vietnamese-speaking woman with cholelithiasis (surgery deferred), CKD III, HTN, and HLD, presenting with severe acute biliary pancreatitis (lipase 603, biliary dilatation on CT, hyperbilirubinemia) complicated by suspected biliary sepsis (T 101.3, leukocytosis, hypotension responsive to fluids) and AKI on CKD (Cr 1.8 from baseline 1.3).

PROBLEM-BASED PLAN:

#1 SEVERE ACUTE BILIARY PANCREATITIS + SUSPECTED BILIARY SEPSIS
- NPO; IVF aggressive resuscitation with isotonic crystalloid (250 cc/h LR continued, reassess after 4-6h).
- Pip/tazo 3.375 g IV q8h (renally dose-adjusted; reviewed PCN allergy → mild childhood rash, OK).
- GI consult — for possible urgent ERCP within 24-48 hours given biliary obstruction + sepsis.
- General surgery consult for cholecystectomy planning.
- Strict I/O.
- Reassess for ICU upgrade if persistent hypotension, worsening hypoxemia, or worsening organ dysfunction.

#2 AKI ON CKD
- IVF resuscitation as above.
- Hold ibuprofen, hold lisinopril until renal function recovers.
- Renally dose abx.
- Trend creatinine daily.
- Strict I/O.

#3 RESPIRATORY DISTRESS — hypoxemia on 2L NC, RR 22, crackles
- Currently O2 sat 94% on 2L NC. Etiologies on differential include ARDS in setting of severe pancreatitis, aspiration during emesis, pulmonary edema from aggressive resuscitation. Will monitor closely.
- HOB up 30°, telemetry, continuous SpO2.
- Repeat CXR in AM; consider escalation to ICU if NC requirements increase.

#4 HTN / HLD
- Continue atorvastatin.
- Hold lisinopril during AKI.

#5 DM PRE-DIABETES / HYPERGLYCEMIA
- Anticipate stress-related hyperglycemia and steroid effect (one dose ED pre-med). Sliding-scale insulin per glucose monitoring.

#6 DVT / VTE PROPHYLAXIS
- Mechanical compression (SCDs) — given pancreatitis with potential progression to procedures.
- Hold pharmacologic prophylaxis pending consideration of urgent ERCP.

#7 NUTRITION
- NPO initially; will reintroduce when pain improves and ileus resolves. Nutrition consult.

#8 PAIN
- IV morphine 2-4 mg q4h PRN (avoid NSAIDs given AKI). Will reassess.

#9 LATENT TB INFECTION
- Documented in chart, no symptoms of active TB on history. Will monitor; no current changes.

#10 COMMUNICATION / FAMILY
- Vietnamese interpreter mandatory for all substantive conversations. Son Quang (English-fluent) primary family contact. Family meeting tonight to confirm understanding.
- Verbal healthcare proxy designation: son Quang.
- Code status: FULL CODE (confirmed with patient and family through interpreter).

Documented at bedside by Dr. Hong (PGY-3) at 10:30 PM with Dr. Beckman attending review. Total documentation + history-taking time: 110 minutes (interpreter time 45 minutes).
attestation:
Electronically signed by:
Dr. Marcus Hong, MD (PGY-3, Internal Medicine) — primary author
Dr. Jennifer Beckman, MD (Attending, Internal Medicine) — reviewing attending
Dr. Kavita Patel, MD (Emergency Medicine) — ED note co-signed separately
Date: 2/15/2027
